speaker: interviewer
text: It's the 16th of April, 1996. Shmuel, let's go back to the yeshiva. to [NON-ENGLISH]. Did you find your studies intellectually satisfying?

speaker: interviewee
text: Challenging, I would say more than satisfying.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you really enjoyed it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Challenging.

speaker: interviewer
text: Challenging.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember it was challenging. I enjoyed-- I don't know, the words in German did not exist for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you find it intellectually exciting?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, because at the age of 12, I suddenly found myself in a different atmosphere. And the level of learning was much more demanding.

speaker: interviewer
text: You obviously were with a very gifted group of boys as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and I was youngest as far as I remember. But it was challenging. It was not easy. But challenging is a good word for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you enjoyed the challenge? You found the challenge to your liking?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you rate with your classmates? Did you find that you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I was a star.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were the star?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not the star, a star. I don't want the star. But in my class, there were about, I don't know, 50 or 60-- I couldn't tell you exactly. I was definitely a star.

speaker: interviewer
text: You lived there on the premises?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was boarding-- so-called boarding school, old-fashioned boarding school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What time did you start your studies?

speaker: interviewee
text: Early in the morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: How early?

speaker: interviewee
text: As early as possible. 6 o'clock in the morning. In winter it was dark.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when would you have finished?

speaker: interviewee
text: Late-- 10 o'clock.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you studied all day.

speaker: interviewee
text: All day.

speaker: interviewer
text: All day and half the night?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you mentioned earlier that you visited a relative occasionally on Shabbat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was your only recreation?

speaker: interviewee
text: They word recreation I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your only time out from the yeshiva?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you studied at the yeshiva, did you have access to knowledge other than Jewish religious studies?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was always politically minded. I remember that I could not afford newspapers. And I used to go to a corner where they sold newspapers, and used to pay a couple of grosh, whatever it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: The newspapers were clipped so people should be able to open it and read it without paying. See. This was an invention that was made in Poland a long time before here. So that person who sold them, used to have one or two open. And they used to give him something. And I used to read, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was my father says, not very legal. He didn't like it. But I was interested, so I very political minded, besides from my sisters, I got it, you know-

speaker: interviewer
text: So that left wing influence of your sisters--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, penetrated little by little. Because I remember in the '30s was the Spanish Civil War. And I, at that time already, sided with the left, with the Republicans. So this gives me an idea at the age of 13--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were starting to develop--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was develop--

speaker: interviewer
text: --secular opinions.

speaker: interviewee
text: --secular opinions, political. I was political minded early then, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware that at the same time as you were discovering about the Spanish Civil War the other things that were happening in Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this discussed amongst the boys at the yeshiva?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never, never.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was a secret at the yeshiva?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was my secret.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father know about--

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely not. He wouldn't have condoned it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you never found amongst the boys at the yeshiva someone you could trust sufficiently to discuss these kind of things with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe there were some. But I was-- I'm a loner all my life, till today. I live a lonely life, not-- not with my wife and children. But I'm not very social. But from what I remember, their interests were not my interests.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay at the yeshiva?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What years were they? From when till when?

speaker: interviewer
text: What made you leave the yeshiva?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. Honest. To tell you honestly, I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you left?

speaker: interviewee
text: I left on my own volition, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You decided--

speaker: interviewee
text: I decided this was not my way of life.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was a very radical departure from your destined role.

speaker: interviewee
text: See, if-- if there would be excommunication in Jewish life, I was-- I would be doomed to excommunication. But luckily, Jewish people cannot be excommunicated.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you just walked out?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't go back to-- to yeshiva.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: For a living? Exist?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go home?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My father wouldn't have tolerated it. I remember vaguely, that I had a clash with my father. That was the first time and the last time in my life. But always-- I always accepted everything was dished out to me. And I remember the saying when I left home, and I said, my foot will never step in here again. At the age of 14. And I never came in again.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you said, I will never come back here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not back here. That was addressed to my father. So I never stepped in the house again. That was the last time I with my parents-- with my father together.

speaker: interviewer
text: You never had any communication again with your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: None whatsoever. None whatsoever.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was the last word you spoke to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: The last word I spoke to him. He ignored me, and I accepted it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean the day-- the day when I left?

speaker: interviewer
text: Once you left home?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I left home, I took a train and went to my father's brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your uncle?

speaker: interviewee
text: My uncle.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Warsaw, who was as religious as my father, or even more maybe. And I told some fib that yeshiva is closed. I don't remember exactly. But I remember this night I slept there. And early in the morning, I left because I was afraid my father would come to take-- fetch me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went, my mother had a sister in a city called Lodz. It's the largest-- second largest city in Poland. And I was there for quite a number of weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Living with your auntie?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you try to go and find work? What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't have had any chance, because at that time, work-- to get work was not that simple. After a while, I-- what I'm saying now is-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I remember vaguely. I don't remember exactly-- I'm 73 now. That was-- what is it? 14, and 59 years. I remember I went back to Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: After you stayed with your aunt?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, till the rage subsided from my father. I was in communication with my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, how did you communicate with your mother during all of this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I cannot answer because I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: But there was contact?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, with mother. I had never any conflict with my mother till the last day when I saw her, never any conflict whatsoever.

speaker: interviewer
text: So once you returned to Warsaw, what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: I started working. I remembered the first work was that I carried coal. The basket with coals I used to carry. [? And ?] [INAUDIBLE] $0.02 for sustenance.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you-- you worked carrying coal?

speaker: interviewee
text: Coal, this was for a while. And then I went to work in a sweatshop. So, I don't know. Can I use that term?

speaker: interviewer
text: Absolutely.

speaker: interviewee
text: In a sweatshop. Let me try to describe what I did. That was some textile [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] jackets or sweaters. Yes, I don't know the English word. Sweater, jumpers. And I remember it was some kind of machines that used to make it like Angora. You just had to--

speaker: interviewer
text: It fluffied it up.

speaker: interviewee
text: Fluffied it up. Good, thank you. That was a good expression. I like it. Fluffied it up. And I fluffed it up [INAUDIBLE] the evening, till-- till the boss said go home.

speaker: interviewer
text: An infinite number of hours? Whatever the boss said.

speaker: interviewee
text: What the boss said. No such thing. The union didn't exist.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you stop carrying the coal, and worked in the sweat shop?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was a better job?

speaker: interviewee
text: A better job, yes. Easier physically, definitely. And I-- I earned 5 zlotys a week as far as I remember. And at that time an American dollar was 5 zloty. So you can imagine I made an American dollar a week. That was my--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember the very beginning. But I don't know if-- it come-- it came now a time when we had a great upheaval in our home. And I would rather not-- not go into particulars, because after I left home, the whole family was involved in that so-called break up. And I would like just to leave it at that.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now while you were working, what happened to these left-wing tendencies of yours that had began with your sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never developed-- never developed any more than just sympathy. I was never active, I mean, in any-- you have to realize that the Communist Party was illegal in Poland, when just for being a member of any activity we went to jail.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this would have been a highly subversive--

speaker: interviewee
text: Subversive. And I was not for that-- made up for that kind of life. My older sister was a member. And she sat in jail.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your sister went to jail--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --for her political views.

speaker: interviewee
text: Political activities, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, tell me. We are talking about post-1937. We're talking about which years?

speaker: interviewee
text: From '37 to '39 of when the Second World War break out-- broke out, sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you know about what was happening politically now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I knew-- I remember in 1933, when I was 10 years old, I remember that Hitler came to power.

speaker: interviewer
text: You remember that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, I remember. Yes. I remember very definitely. And I remember that I was so keen on leaving Poland, because-- I couldn't anticipate, of course, the Holocaust. But I knew the disaster was coming-- disaster. Hitler never made a secret of it that he was so vehemently anti-Jewish. He didn't, of course, say he was going to burn us alive. He sprang a surprise in this respect. But I knew dangerous, at age of 10, I knew that enormous danger was coming. Terrible danger was coming. But was nothing--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do? Did you discuss this with your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. But we were helpless, couldn't do anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even had you wanted-- had your parents wanted to-- there was nothing you could?

speaker: interviewee
text: You couldn't go anywhere. Nobody would let you in.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in the years subsequent to that, as you grew older, and now as you were working in the sweatshop, you followed the events that were occurring in Germany and Austria--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. Anschluss, Crystal Night, Anschluss, Crystal Night, in chronological order. Anschluss was with Austria. Crystal Night was in Germany. The invasion of Czechoslovakia. Of course I remember everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel that it was only a short question of time--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes--

speaker: interviewer
text: --before you would be--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes--

speaker: interviewer
text: --'you' meaning the Poles, Poland--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Would be invaded, yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you think that everybody knew that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am not so sure about that.

speaker: interviewer
text: But that was certainly your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Mine, because I am-- I was Polish, into [INAUDIBLE] politics, and I could see the dark clouds.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the arrival of the Germans in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Warsaw. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And the bombing started.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was your first--

speaker: interviewee
text: First.

speaker: interviewer
text: --your first inclination that there was a change.

speaker: interviewee
text: The war-- not a change. The war broke out without announcing, you know. Nobody knew anything. I remember people saying these are just exercises.

speaker: interviewer
text: Suddenly you heard this incredible noise?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes bombing, bomb the city on Warsaw. And I remember coming to the railway station. It was hard to get a train back to Falenica.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went back to Falenica?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Where my mother was, my sisters were. But that lasted two or three weeks, as we were on the left side of the Vistula that divided Warsaw in--

speaker: interviewer
text: The Vistula being the big river?

speaker: interviewee
text: The river, yes. The Germans marched in-- into that Falenica place about seven or eight days before Warsaw was taken. And that-- they set afire Falenica. And we were sitting in the cellar.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was sitting in the cellar?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, my sisters, and I. My brother stayed with his father. And I remember I was thinking the choice of either being burned alive or just jump out and face the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Got out.

speaker: interviewer
text: All of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: All of us. The women were left to go where they wanted. As a matter of fact, my mother, my sisters went to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my mother went to her sister that lived in that place called Otwock, not far away, a very short distance. And men, including myself-- I was 16 years old-- were taken [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] for a march-- a long march-- I would say about 30, 40 kilometers-- to a place called Minsk.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were marched to Minsk?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who marched you to Minsk?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Wehrmacht or the SS?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Wehrmacht. I don't think the SS was at that time. I didn't remember the word even exists. The Wehrmacht.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Wehrmacht. The German army.

speaker: interviewee
text: The German army.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say that it marched the men, this was all the men of Falenica, or only--

speaker: interviewee
text: All surrounding, all the surrounding.

speaker: interviewer
text: Poles and Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Every male--

speaker: interviewee
text: That is correct--

speaker: interviewer
text: --of a certain age?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were marched off. How long did it take you to get--

speaker: interviewee
text: It took long time. All night. It took a long time till we arrived in [SIGHS] in Minsk. And we were all marched into a place where they used to exercise horses.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you kept in this place?

speaker: interviewee
text: A few days, a few days.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened while you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: While we were there, we were hungry, and we were beaten by the Poles.

speaker: interviewer
text: So even within this group of prisoners--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --there was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --a Polish group and a Jewish group? That was with the group. The Jews didn't beat. We were very docile. But I remember when they let me out, I was blood at my head. So you were bashed up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bashed up.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after you had been there for a few days, you were just let go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Let go. I went to the place where my mother was staying.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your sisters with your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think so. I'm not sure. I couldn't say this. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you remain with your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: A few weeks. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were on our way to Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: To Russia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The beginning-- the part of Poland that was occupied by Russia, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: To escape the Nazis. Escape the Nazis--

speaker: interviewer
text: --trying to move into the part of Poland that was now controlled by Russia.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to Bialystok.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you went to Bialystok.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Large city.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do in Bialystok?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wait for-- wait to be registered, and sended-- send away with the Russians to another place.

speaker: interviewer
text: So were there many other Jewish people like you who were fleeing--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in Bialystok, how long were you kept. Or how long did you stay?

speaker: interviewee
text: No I was free. I wasn't kept. It was a few weeks, I was there. I couldn't remember exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: With your mom?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My mom was still in Poland under German occupation. So she came a few weeks later.

speaker: interviewer
text: And ultimately, your mother joined you in Bialystok?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in Bialystok. She joined me in a city called Babruysk. This is-- Belarus it's called now.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went from Bialystok--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- I didn't go. I was taken, registered and sent away to a city.

speaker: interviewer
text: By the Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: By the Russians, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were sent to Babruysk?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And there I worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of work did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Timber mills.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do in the timber mills?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was a large factory that made doors and windows. I was just put in a place and-- and worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: And once you were in Babruysk--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --your mother joined you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Later, yes, a few weeks later.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister went to another place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now you had two sisters.

speaker: interviewee
text: Two sisters, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: They both went to different places?

speaker: interviewee
text: They both went to different places, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So initially in Babruysk, there was you and then--

speaker: interviewee
text: Mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now while all this was happening, where was your father and your older brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: They remained--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Falenica, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: They remained?

speaker: interviewee
text: Stayed all through the bitter end.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they remained in Falenica while the other part of the family traveled east?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now what were conditions like in Babruysk?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tough, like in Soviet Russia. The basics, not more than the basics. Very tough, the basics.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had-- where did you live when you were in the timber factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the begin-- at the beginning, before my mother came, I was together with other single people, eight or 10 in a place where we just had a place to sleep.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. The basics. Yes, we didn't suffer from any shortage of food.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the work like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Work was hard work for a boy of 16.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hard, physical labor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hard, physical labor, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your feelings towards the Russians now that you were living in an area controlled by--

speaker: interviewee
text: At the begin-- at the beginning very grateful, because I escaped the Nazis. At the beginning, very grateful.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you saw them at this time--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --as your saviors?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in terms of the communist ideology, how did you feel about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I started seeing the false, but you cannot express yourself in Russia. They used to say in Russia words-- I don't know where we heard a joke-- that in Soviet Russia, the only place you can open your mouth is at the dentist.

speaker: interviewer
text: So whatever faults and failings you might have seen--

speaker: interviewee
text: Start observing, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --they were kept very quiet.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Kept it to myself. Kept it to myself. No jokes, no sayings, no criticisms, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now your mother eventually joined you in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Joined me in Babruysk, yes. By that time we got a little room. It was a long corridor, you know, with single rooms. And that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were with your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: No sisters yet?

speaker: interviewee
text: They arrived much later. I couldn't remember when. But they arrived much later.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long were you in Babruysk?

speaker: interviewee
text: From October '39 till June '41.

speaker: interviewer
text: All the time working in the timber mill?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the time working in the timber, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Once your sisters joined or came to Babruysk, did you still live in the same place?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I don't remember having-- till I escaped, till the Nazis marched in and the beginning of the real war in '41-- having been out of this place. A small, one room.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when your sisters arrived, they worked? They found work?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you mentioned that you remained in Babruysk until exactly when?

speaker: interviewee
text: The war broke out in 1941, the 22nd of June. And I left on 27th.

speaker: interviewer
text: Five days later.

speaker: interviewee
text: Five days later we heard that the Nazis are coming in. And whoever can save, my mother told me, to leave.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was your mother who said to you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, leave because you--

speaker: interviewer
text: --go away.

speaker: interviewee
text: Go away. Maybe you will save yourself.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother made no attempt to go with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was so sad. And we didn't believe that the Russians would be defeated so quickly. They always presented themselves as a mighty power. And suddenly the-- the Germans are coming close and closer. So I ran away. Actually I ran 120 kilometers.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean run physically?

speaker: interviewee
text: Run physically--

speaker: interviewer
text: On foot?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, on foot. Yeah, of course, on foot.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: To a city called Mogilev.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is Mogilev?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Belarus.

speaker: interviewer
text: Still part of Belarus.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of city is Mogilev?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, gosh. What can I to tell you? I remember only coming-- arriving with sores on my feet from running so much, and coming to the train, and pushing myself in the cattle trains, trying to escape.

speaker: interviewer
text: These cattle trains were Russian trains?

speaker: interviewee
text: --taking out people who have wanted to escape further inside Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who were the people you found yourself with?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jews, non-Jews, anybody?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. On the whole there were more Jewish than non-Jewish. People who are political affiliated, non-Jews were running as well, because they were afraid more.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so people who were known pro-Communists were going further into Russia?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is correct. And Jews, because they were-- because they were Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what happened to those people who didn't go east?

speaker: interviewee
text: Didn't go west, you mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: East-- further towards Russia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, further towards Russia. What happened to them? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't have exact reports. But what I heard from Babruysk, from what I heard later, they were put into wooden houses and burned alive. You have to realize one things. The Jews-- even the Jews who were in different categories, the ones who were living not under communist regime were tortured to death. The ones who were living in Russia were immediately killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were sort of different types of treatment--

speaker: interviewee
text: Different approach--

speaker: interviewer
text: --for different types of Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: These were killed slowly. These one were killed immediately.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now your mother and your sisters--

speaker: interviewee
text: All perished. Never heard of them any more, anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you believe that they were part of this group of people who were--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't believe. I know for sure. They didn't have anywhere to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so they perished in these flames?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your father and brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: As far as I know, he-- he-- there was an action in Falenica in 19-- in September '42. And they were sent to Treblinka.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the day that you left Babruysk was the last day--

speaker: interviewee
text: Last day I had any-- seen any family, close family, in my life, at the ripe age of 18.

speaker: interviewer
text: So once you got into Mogilev, what-- on this train, on this cattle train-- what happened when you arrived there?

speaker: interviewee
text: My memory is fading. I don't remember exactly. I know that they took us-- the train went further, deep into Russia to city called Tambov.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was Tambov?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tambov was central Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long would you have been on this train?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. Days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Several days?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Definitely days. It was start and stop, and collecting more people, you see.

speaker: interviewer
text: With opportunity for food and drink occasionally? Do you remember being hungry and thirsty?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the time, but-- there was so much suffering. There was no air, and no water, and no food, so-- was taken. At that time, actually, we were so afraid, that we didn't know what to-- what to fear first, and what to suffer first.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were frightened the Germans would catch up with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the lack of food and drink was a secondary worry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That-- that came later.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you arrived in Tambov--

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] From Tambov, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was sent to a city called Magnitogorsk.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is Magnitogorsk?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ural. Ural mountains, you know. The--

speaker: interviewer
text: In the Ural Mountains?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ural Mountains, on the way to Siberia, before Siberia, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of city is Magnitogorsk?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was a large city that worked only for military purposes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Manufacturing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What sort of things?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Russia, you don't know-- you don't know these things. [LAUGHING] I didn't-- didn't go to ask them, what do you do?

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was well known. A large city-- actually, this city was built by the Communists. A new city in the Urals was built.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was created--

speaker: interviewee
text: Created.

speaker: interviewer
text: Purpose--

speaker: interviewee
text: Purpose.

speaker: interviewer
text: Created city.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: To-- to create armaments--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --for the Russian war effort.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is where you found yourself when?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say by then it was mid-summer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of the year?

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's stop tape.
